This group has been so helpful to me so I decided to share my experience. Picture is of foot after Topaz surgery.
I have had PF for about a year. I also have bunions. In February I had a bunionectomy on one foot and Topaz surgery on both feet. It has now been six months and I thought I would give a review of the  Topaz surgery.
At first it was very painful. More painful than the bunionectomy! It took weeks to heal the 25 holes I had in each heel. About 6 weeks after the surgery I had a really hard time walking. It was almost unbearable. I talked with my Podiatrist and she said healing from the bunionectomy was throwing off my posture and possibly making everything worse. She said give it 6 months.  My PF is definitely better now. Almost gone in one foot. But I am not 100% sure it was the Topaz surgery that helped.
Like most of you I have spent hundreds of dollars on shoes and products to help my plantar fasciitis. Ultimately, for me, I think correcting my anterior pelvic tilt and poor posture are what have helped the most. I wear night splints also and I am very careful how I position my feet when sitting. I find sitting in the car very painful. I have a foot rest under my desk and that helps a lot! I have also worn Crocs almost exclusively for the past couple months. They cushion my feet and give me relief. Exercising and stretches have helped as well.
I am not completely healed but I feel I am getting close. My right foot will go hours and sometimes almost all day without pain! See less

I had to quit snowboarding after 25 years of riding due to an injury that lead to metatarsalgia and nerve damage exacerbated by hammer toe.  The pressure on my feet from the stiff boots, tight bindings and forces from pulling edges on the board were just too much!  Then I found bi-level snowskates.
I can wear normal, soft and slightly oversized boots with custom insoles.  (See photo comparing painful snowboard boots to my snowskate boots). I am not strapped into bindings.  To lift an edge of the sub deck on the snow you just lean and the downward force on the wider upper deck lifts the opposing edge of the board on the snow.
This completely changed my world and allows me to continue to “snowboard” with no more pain than I’m used to living with everyday. You go a little slower (by natural choice), so it makes greens feel like blues and blues feel like blacks.  You can have a blast all day on a gentle green!
Almost all ski areas in Colorado allow snowskates, except the one my family has been riding for 27 years.  I’m trying to get it approved by them as an adaptive device to accommodate my impairment, since I otherwise cannot snowboard or ski anymore.  Wish me luck, but that’s not why I’m sharing.
Most folks see my snowskate and think I’m just some middle-aged skater punk that won’t grow up.  While that may be true, it’s not why I quit snowboarding after 25 years and started snowskating.  This is genuinely used by me to accommodate my foot pain and let me stay on the slopes.  Maybe it can help you too.
It takes a little time to learn, but then it carves and stops just like a snowboard.
Mine is a Buckshot from Hovland Snowskates. I’m not associated with this company.  I’m just a snowboarder with bad feet that found a solution that works for me.
I wish you all foot relief in whatever strange way you can find it. See less

I wanted to post an update on my experience with bunion surgery for anyone considering it, hopefully it will provide some optimism!
I had lapiplasty on my left foot in August 2024. At six months I had an asymptomatic nonunion aka it felt fine but the bones didn’t appear fused. Also had CT scan to confirm this nonunion.
Due to the nonunion I was hesitant about doing my right foot but the pain from the deformity was significant so I had the same procedure on the right foot in October 2025. I had a significantly greater amount of post-operative pain for no known reason and needed much more pain meds on the right foot than I did the left, and I had wound healing issues (dehiscence and took months to completely close) with the biggest incision on the right foot. The wound eventually healed but the scar looks a little weird.
However as of yesterday I got these x-rays and both the right foot and the left foot are healed and fused. The left one just took longer I guess. I am pain-free and released from the surgeon’s care unless I have future issues. He said if the hardware ever bothers me he can take it out and the bunion correction will stay due to the bones being fused.
It was a long and difficult road over about 20 months but I’m very happy with my results and would do it again if I had to go back in time. I was 33 when I started the process and I wanted to get them fixed while I was still relatively young. I chose this procedure because of the low recurrence rate and because I felt like it addressed the root cause of the deformity. I hope this is helpful for anyone considering it! See less

body:
Today is Day 6 after my foot surgery.
My surgeon prescribed up to 8 oxycodone tablets per day, but I only took 4 tablets on Day 4 and none today. Thankfully, the swelling has gone down significantly, and my pain has been manageable.
I have a little itching around the surgical area, which seems to be part of the healing process. I’ve been eating lots of fruit every day, so I haven’t experienced any constipation since the surgery.
I’m using a bedside commode when needed and a walker instead of crutches, which feels safer and more comfortable for me right now.
I’m also taking Vitamin C, calcium, B-complex, and Vitamin D regularly to support healing.
Feeling grateful for the progress so far and looking forward to a full recovery.
See less

body:
UPDATE... been to Northwich foot clinic, feeling very positive, got insoles with wedges attached after watching myself walk I can understand why the bursa is not getting better
I've been getting so frustrated not being able to get outdoors for a decent walk .
I've been diagnosed with Adventitial bursitis involving the plantar surface at the level of the 5th metatarsal head , ive never had problems before and had a tailors bunion for many years, my foot is very painful when exercising and  wearing any type of  footwear hurts the top and side of my foot .
I'm wondering if anybody else had similar and how to help it. ??
.  I'm still waiting for an orthopaedic specialist appointment on the NHS which could take up to 52 weeks.
I've tried RICE, paracetamol,  ibuprofen, compresses, rock tape, toe separators, heat , ibuprofen gels, tens machine, foot spa, massagers, new insoles, expensive socks etc. Nothing helps
I'm frightened that I will never get back out there. See less

Anybody do Dr Angela’s program and it didn’t work?
I’m considering ordering the whole system but don’t want to get burned again. I’ve had this for 1.5 years in both feet with little improvement here and there short term and always comes back with a vengeance. Already tried 5 shockwave sessions based on podiatrist recommendations. Tried PT with little real results. Tried peptides for 12 weeks which didn’t seem to do anything. Went full Keto/carnivore to help with inflammation but obviously it’s beyond inflammation and is faciosis at this point. Tried different shoes, recovery slides, night splints, etc etc … losing hope

comments: View more answers Hollister Holiday The one thing that has helped me is minimal drop shoes with wide toe box. Topo Ultra fly 6. And wide toe socks! The next thing is a PT that understands it starts at the low back, hips, hamstrings, calf’s, and ankles. And does measurements of your ankle movements and does exam of how tight your hips, and legs are. Along with your “gait” sometimes. Find a good massage therapist and get a myofascial release massages. You can google “the sling method” it’s for runners but it’s helpful information. I do Dr Angela exercises. Buy a tool on amazon and scape your feet and calf’s! I would not do shots! They do have a new surgery procedure called PFE. Plantar Fascia Embolization And another one is Gastrocnemius Recession You can Google these if they fit your situation.

View all 3 replies Answer as Justin Seth Kinney posted to Plantar Fasciitis Talk and Tips Support Group Dry needling or Shockwave? I have been dealing with this for a full year now and tried all the shoes, trinkets, etc and now have had a few sessions with PT after a visit to the podiatrist. PT suggested dry needling, podiatrist suggested shockwave. They of course just happen to offer the things they recommend but both are similar price and timelines to recovery from what I can tell. Any first hand experience is helpful. For additional context, this started as achillies tendinitis which would come and go and this would go away with a couple days of elevated feet. Now my calves stay chronically tight and never seem to stay loose despite stretching.
Comment Dane Burmester All-star contributor Do shockwave.
View all 3 replies Sarah Price-Balfour IASTM could be beneficial for your calves. Might be worth a Google
View 1 reply Answer as Justin

body:
Frustrated. A friend referred me to a large orthopedic office. Was seen by a podiatrist instead of an ortho Dr. They took X-rays. Podiatrist came in, glanced at X-rays, pushed on my heel. Advised I have plantar fasciitis and pronation. He said he’ll give me cortisone injections and orthotic shoe inserts. I asked about PRP therapy or peptides (like the poster in their lobby). He said those don’t work. I said it seems like most who get cortisone’s get temporary relief, and some report it’s even worse after. He said you came in for my opinion. Come back if you want injections, got up and left. See less

body:
I have tried literally almost everything to help heal my feet from plantar fasciitis. I had 3 steroid injections 2 of them worked temporarily. I have done PT, gone through multiple types of shoes, certain creams and castor oil, dry needling, the stretches and foot exercises foot compression sleeves, toe spreaders, arch supports, night splints and I have yet to have more than 3 months of relief in about 6 years.
I was wondering if foot and calf massages and acupuncture would be good next steps in trying to heal my feet?? Any information will be helpful! See less

body:
Bunion and hammertoe surgery on 1/28. Am I crazy for thinking my big toe is turning in again?? I’m so disappointed. Foot is still crazy swollen.
All reactions:
comments: View more answers RubyGrape9082 It can happen from anesthesia

Judy Shearer Luce Rising contributor It’s a normal process after a traumatic event usually happens about 3 to 4 months afterwards. I’m a hairdresser and I’ve seen it happen to so many people. The technical word is telogen Eluvium. Diffuse hair thinning due to surgery anesthesia childbirth, low, iron stress, etc. it is self limiting and will correct and things will get back to normal.

Answer as Justin Meghan Shriver posted to bunion surgery / foot surgery support group All-star contributor Bunion and hammertoe surgery on 1/28. Am I crazy for thinking my big toe is turning in again?? I’m so disappointed. Foot is still crazy swollen. All reactions:
Comment View more answers Beverley Brown Rising contributor Good grief it looks so good wouldn't think you'd had an op 5 months ago

Nelly Starfall You are not crazy for noticing that, but at this stage it is very easy for swelling and positioning changes to create an illusion that something is shifting when it actually is not. After bunion and hammertoe surgery, especially in the first couple of months, the foot is still very swollen and the soft tissues are tight and uneven. That can pull the toe slightly, make it look rotated, or change how it rests depending on the time of day, footwear, or even how long you have been on your feet. A lot of people also underestimate how much swelling can distort alignment visually. Even when the bone correction is holding, the surrounding tissue can temporarily “mask” the final result. It is usually too early to judge long term alignment when swelling is still described as “crazy swollen.” What helped me understand these confusing stages of recovery was ESSENTIALS OF JOINT HEALTH by Joe Zapata. It is not just a general recovery book, it explains how bones, joints, and soft tissue all interact during healing and why appearance and function often lag behind the actual surgical correction. Since you are still early in recovery, this is definitely something worth mentioning at your follow up, but what you are seeing can still fall within normal post op changes. You can find ESSENTIALS OF JOINT HEALTH by Joe Zapata on Amazon. It is especially helpful for understanding why swelling can temporarily change how your foot looks even when healing is on track.

UpDate:  it’s been 2 weeks since my surgery
And I promised I would give an update throughout my recovery so here we go. ￼ I got my stitches out yesterday it was painless, I am in a small boot the one you see in the pictures below I am allowed to start bearing weight slowly using my crutches and add weight as I feel comfortable. I have to sleep in a night splint. Over all this far my recovery has not been too bad and my pain has been very minimal I do feel some pain now as I put weight on my foot but I just do it slowly a little bit at a time. My feelings this far is so far so good I’m glad I did the surgery. Thank you everyone for the support I really appreciate it.￼￼￼￼
Hi everyone I’ve waited to make this post because I wanted to give a real honest opinion￼/update I had surgery on Friday the 20th Plantar fascial ￼release. The actual surgery￼ took about 45 minutes it was done as an out patient. I was sent home with aspirin, pain meds, my foot was wrapped and in a small boot. They done a nerve￼ block to help with pain control for the first 24 hours. My instructions were to be￼ non-weight-bearing for two weeks Elevate and ice to help with swelling Also to do leg raises knee bends and toe stretches open and close nothing too hard just very gentle exercises￼ ,they sent me home with crutches which so far have been the worst part of this surgery I’m very independent and used to doing things on my own so it’s hard to ask my husband to help me with things.￼ I was told I could change my foot wrap and take a shower on Monday which was yesterday so about four days after surgery I could shower￼. I only had swelling on day one so I did use ice all day Friday into Saturday after that I’ve not had any more swelling. There is two small incisions on either side of my foot a couple stitches on both sides and very little bruising￼. I go back in two weeks to get my stitches out and I will probably be able to start bearing weight on my foot we will see so far what I feel seems to be promising only time will tell if the surgery was a success or not but so far I’m happy I made the decision to go ahead and do the surgery.￼￼ I hope this helps anyone who has questions about the surgery this is just My experience with it so far￼. See less

Day 13 and stitches out.  Overall it truly has not be as bad as I thought.  I truly believe elevation and ice behind my knee is the key.  This is what was done:
. 1)Distal modified McBride bunionectony with Lapidus procedure
2)corrective osteotomy 2nd metatarsal with internal fixation
3)resection arthoplasty and pinning right 2nd hammertoe.

I’m 50 and based in London, UK, and I finally have my MIS bunion surgery scheduled for the end of this month. After consulting with two different surgeons, we agreed that MIS was the most suitable option for me.
My bunion is moderate rather than severe, my toe still has good mobility, and my soft tissue is healthy — all indicators that the deformity can be corrected safely through tiny incisions without the larger exposure required in open surgery.

Hello! Has anyone managed to reverse a tailors’ bunion without surgery?
All reactions:

Hi there new to the group! I have bunions on both feet about to have surgery on one
just curious what type of pain bunions cause u? My entire foot hurts all the time!
All reactions:
comments: Linda Chelle Lyman I have terrible calluses on my left foot that feel like I have rocks in my shoe. It's all due to my toes criss crossing. The top of my foot also hurts all the time. At times it feels like it's on fire. The bunion itself rarely hurts. I've put off surgery for 20 years. Missing 8 weeks of work spends just as painful.

Ang Morris I am just like you! Bunions both feet, but my left foot is much worse - I need surgery very badly, but due to other physical conditions, the surgeon is very concerned/hesitant to do the bunion surgery as I can’t be mobile safely after with the boot & that knee rolling/walker thingy. What is shocking is …..they didn’t tell me anything to do since can’t have surgery…..it really hurts & I don’t know how I’m going to deal with it long term especially knowing I don’t have surgery. On my foot, the moderate bunion means I’d have to have the surgeon cut off part of bones from my big toe & then other toes to insert hardware. He said most people only have to do this to the big toe, but due to the level of my bunion & the side toes bending/arching that is why I’d have hardware into 3 toes My pain is like my bones are crossing/crunching/crowded. Charlie horse type cramps. Sometimes hurts to put weight on & wakes me up. Inflammation. Actual side of toe/foot where bunion actually is can get very sore & painful. I really hope your surgery goes GREAT - prayers it goes as smoothly as possible & as little pain as possible
Answer as Justin

body:
Just wondering if anyone here has had hardware removed? And if so what was their experience and recovery like?
I'm 5 months post revision surgery and everytime I weight-bear my foot hurts a lot, I'm almost certain at this point it's a hardware issue (I have 14 screws and 2 plates between my first and second metatarsal) and I'll most likely need to have it removed so trying to see what others experiences were like.

10 days postop. Had my appointment with my podiatrist today and was fitted for a boot. My initial thought was that it looks worse however, my doctor ensured me that I was healing nicely. No sign of infection and my stitches are dissolvable, which is a relief that I will not have to have them removed. Weight-bearing as tolerated for short distances, so I'll be keeping the scooter around if needed. I do have to say my doctor is wonderful and did reassure me that taking one more week off of work is OK. Looks like three more weeks in the boot and then transitioning to shoes just in time for the snow to fall.
See less
All reactions:

Betty Ann Mellon Egolf replied Amanda Morrison Top contributor I only wore a boot for two weeks. It started hurting a bit a few months after and still looked like a bunion. comments: View more answers Jenn Chladil Top contributor Mine peeled off after the swelling went down. I don't know if that is normal or not. It hasn't come back as my gait is different. Plus I am wearing shoes with a wide toe box. My surgeon didn't take it off. I should have asked him to and he probably would have. Maybe ask your surgeon if that can be done. Best to you!

Sara Powning All-star contributor I haven’t had surgery, but here to say my calluses have changed (are lessened) since I started wearing wide toe box Altra Running Shoes as my daily footwear. I think the toe spread has helped. It’s likely that if you work on your gait at anytime (which will help prevent bunion recurrence after surgery too) and wear footwear with toe splay space— then calluses will probably change and hopefully be reduced. I had the callus under the ball of my foot described in the Gait Happens video linked (this callus is now essentially gone with wearing wide toe box footwear!) and also a “pinch callus” on the side of my big toe. Are you talking about either of these? https://www.instagram.com/reel/C5l0IGAPSf9/?igsh=MXVvdGx2eWxrNmlodQ== FACEBOOK Do you or your patients have pain/calluses in the ball of the foot? 👣 Toe Gripping or Flexor Stabilization in gait could be one of the contributing factors.  ➡️ When we grip with the tips of our toes, we put pressure only through the tips of the toes and the ball of the foot, rather than distributing the weight evenly across the forefoot.  Now add this motion into push off 🚶‍♀️where we have increased load moving through the forefoot and it's not surprising that our tissues start yapping at us!!  ⭐️📢 If you want to learn more about forefoot rocker and aberrant loading join us for our FREE webinar, Rock This Way, on April 23rd. Link to register is in our bio 📢 ⭐️ 💪 So.. if we can encourage flattening of the toes by engaging flexor digitorum brevis, we can redistribute that weight for a more stable AND more comfortable pushoff.  Try these three exercises to get you started. #gaithappens #metatarsalgia #lowerextremity #dpt #physicaltherapycontinuingeducation #chiro #movementpro #gaitanalysis Do you or your patients have pain/calluses in the ball of the foot? 👣 Toe Gripping or Flexor Stabilization in gait could be one of the contributing factors.  ➡️ When we grip with the tips of our toes, we put pressure only through the tips of the toes and the ball of the foot, rather than distributing the weight evenly across the forefoot.  Now add this motion into push off 🚶‍♀️where we have increased load moving through the forefoot and it's not surprising that our tissues start yapping at us!!  ⭐️📢 If you want to learn more about forefoot rocker and aberrant loading join us for our FREE webinar, Rock This Way, on April 23rd. Link to register is in our bio 📢 ⭐️ 💪 So.. if we can encourage flattening of the toes by engaging flexor digitorum brevis, we can redistribute that weight for a more stable AND more comfortable pushoff.  Try these three exercises to get you started. #gaithappens #metatarsalgia #lowerextremity #dpt #physicaltherapycontinuingeducation #chiro #movementpro #gaitanalysis

body:
Never thought I’d be joining a foot group but here I am
looking for some opinions!
I’m 25 years old with 2 young kids (2&5) with very flat feet and a very sore bunion!!
I’ve always been flat footed but the bunion along with the pain has been increasing in the past 5 years. I saw a podiatrist a little over a year ago who, after doing X-rays, recommended custom orthotics (which I couldn’t afford at the time) and toe cushions, exercises and a brace to wear at night.
I’ve been doing that for the past 18 months pretty much every day. The cramps I get in the sole of my feet after removing the space savers at the end of the day are insane. I use a tennis ball to roll out the cramps but I’m not sure how much more I can handle.
Since it has been over a year I need a new referral to see the podiatrist again, however I’m going to ask for a second opinion.
Long story short my question is, if it’s recommended, should I have the surgery sooner rather than later (or at all)? I do have a job where I am required to be on my feet for %80 of a normal work day, however they have already told me I would be accommodated for. See less
All reactions:
comments: View more answers Charlotte Marie Jackson Follow My advice would be the sooner the better, I put it off for years and ended up getting arthritis in my big toe...I had my bunion surgery this morning

Brant McCartan replied 1 Reply Francine D'Agostino I agree with Cori Weiner.. Invest in good shoes. I would skip the spacers, but massage and stretch stretch your feet. I think the spacers are doing more harm than good. I've had bunions almost all my life and put off surgery until now. ( I'm 72) I had a child to raise and was an art teacher for 40 years.. always on my feet.. It never seemed to be a good time. You have 2 young children and a job that need you. I think you can buy time by buying good quality shoes that fit you correctly. It made the world of difference for me. See if you can find a store near you that caters to people with problem feet. They will measure you correctly and make recommendations. Google " shoes for bunions" that will give you an idea of brands, styles and prices. I waited for years and finally had minimally invasive percutaneous bunion and hammertoe surgery last week. Good luck to you!
Annie Castelnovo-Mcmullen replied Answer as Justin

On May 2nd, the group America First United hosted a conference at Bryn Du Mansion in Granville. AFU is an extension of America First, a nationally known hate group that espouses racism, antisemitism, and misogyny. The conference was billed as being “bipartisan” and issues such as foreign influence and transparency for the Epstein scandal were featured in the conference promotional materials. However, the overriding agenda was not one of policy issues but one of hatred against Jews, Muslims, non-whites, immigrants, homosexuals others that America First insists have no rightful place in America. According to the New York Times, “there was a slew of epithets for homosexuals, slurs for intellectual disabilities, and antisemitic comments” A Far-Right Experiment in Political Unity - The New York Times .
The conference slid under the local media radar, leaving many in Granville unaware that it took place. For those who read the NYT article with disbelief and shock, its occurrence has launched questions about how First Amendment considerations apply to Bryn Du Mansion and the public and private aspects of its programming. If, as a public facility, Bryn Du may not deny groups access based on their belief systems, then presumably the public has a right to know what events take place there, both public and private, and to appropriately exercise their own First Amendment rights. If Bryn Du is private, then it would have the latitude to restrict programming in its bylaws, for example by excluding political events altogether. Many questions remain about the rights and responsibilities of our village facilities. We hope that ongoing dialogue between Bryn Du and the Village of Granville will result in practical, ethical public guidelines with which to navigate future such events. Our elected and appointed officials also need to be more transparent and communicative about that process, and not just hope that this issue remains unnoticed and soon forgotten. Whatever the outcome, Granville residents need to know what took place in our community on May 2nd, and to voice their opinions with local leaders.

Last Wednesday I had a 10 hour shift on my feet and the pain was slightly different. Instead of it being a stabbing sensation in my heal it was … See more
All reactions:

Just want to share with people in case it can help someone.
I’ve been dealing with PF since 2024.
The past 6 months or so I have a lot of leg pain from the knee down.
Kept thinking it was tight calves that come with PF. Despite all the massagers and stretches. The pain doesn’t go away. My legs feel so heavy especially when I lay down at night. I obsess in my head about taking a rolling pin to them (don’t ask me why).
I mentioned it to the doctor who treats my PF with cortisone shots. He said the heavy leg feeling is a red flag and sent me for ultrasounds.
Turn out it’s venous reflux in my legs. They want to do vein ablation. I haven’t decided yet, but wanted to share in case anyone else was dismissing these feelings and thinking it’s due to PF
I had no bulging veins. No discoloration. No signs other than the heavy feeling/pain. I’m only 43..
All reactions:
comments: View more answers Susan Hutt Check the meds you are on. My friend was taking something for cholesterol I believe and the side effect was heavy feeling in legs.

View all 4 replies Courtney Caine Davis All-star contributor I’m a vascular sonographer and my instructor found my venous reflux on me when I was 20! It’s hereditary. Scanned my mom when we found out and she has it too! It happens because the valves don’t touch to close like they should and keep the blood moving in one direction back toward the heart. . I thought my leg pain was just normal! Turns out it’s not. Start wearing compression socks every day that you can. You’ll notice a difference

4 weeks post op Tailor's Bunion revision and my pin came out today!!!! Hallelujah! Didn't feel good being pulled out but it was quick. Still in pain, still not a fully healed bone, still have to use a post op shoe, xray again in 4 weeks. I can slowly being to weighbear but only in the shoe. The hole from the pin and marrow should heal in a few weeks. If the next xray doesn't show full bone healing they will order a bone stimulator. All reactions:

Good afternoon, I'm having a bunionectom and hamertoe ...what are the MUST haves I should have at home!? Please and thank you
All reactions:
comments: View more comments Jimmy Chan I got a popliteal nerve block after the surgery while I was still under anesthesia, I didn't feel my foot until 4 days later and slept fine every night and went back to desk work hours after surgery. Good luck! I hope you get rest.

Michelle Gasca Tarver replied Lisa Horswill I rotated between lying on back with foot raised on pillows and lying on 'good' side with pillow between knees & hurt foot slightly behind on 2 pillows. Gives back a rest. First 2 weeks got about 5 hrs a night but just into wk 3 & had 7.5 hrs sleep last night!! It gets better!

Comment as Justin Michelle Gasca Tarver posted to Bunion Support Group(: Good afternoon, I'm having a bunionectom and hamertoe ...what are the MUST haves I should have at home!? Please and thank you All reactions:

Comment View more answers Pamigo Essentials (in my opinion ) :- wedge for elevation, ice packs, painkillers (paracetamol and ibuprofen), shower seat (often on freecycle), plastic full leg cover for shower. Nice to haves:- full foot cryo cuff for ice water by aircast, back support cushion, trays/overlap table, knee scooter.

Lizzie Gee Definitely a raised foot rest, Bio oil to rub into wound after you have all dressings off, I also bought Nuage daily hydrating cream for dry skin from Home Bargains which was £1.99 or 99p I can’t remember which, moisturising bath or shower gel for you wound once dressings are off, Water wet wipes are invaluable, a bedside drawer or bag with everything you need close at hand, pen, writing pad, tissues, some goodies to cheer you up, books, tv remote etc etc.,lots of paracetamol. Lots of patience during your recovery, especially the first 2 weeks, get plenty of rest, drink lots of water, even though that means more bathroom trips! I’m at 8 weeks now after having a hammer toe pinned and my big toe had the bunion removed and was fused. Take care Michelle and I hope all goes well for you

I went to the dr recently when my foot (15 months post op lapidus bunion removal and big toe osteotomy with 4 screws and a plate). I have been  experiencing pain, numbness, and tingling. I also have what I assumed was a screw coming out- a hard bumpy protrusion that was  getting bigger. He did x-rays and said he wished the problem was as easy to fix as the hardware ￼coming out.
Apparently, I will eventually need surgery again. The big toe osteotomy area is failing and I have permanent nerve damage and loss of almost all big toe movement The protrusion is a growth on the bone.
Anyone have similar issues? See less

comments: View more answers Peggy Jones Pfister All-star contributor How disappointing. I'm so sorry.

Tracey Coulthard All-star contributor I’m so sorry Bethany . Does the surgeon know why or how it happened ? I will say to keep you positive , move forward , keep looking for your light and things will get better I said this in recent post but this particular recovery and anytime I have hardship , difficult situations or whatever…I remind myself of that childhood book I /we we read , depending on your age of course but it’s called “Going on a Lion Hunt “ you can’t go under it , you can’t go over it , you got to go through it . It was the moral of the kids book and who would have thought 55 years later the book resonates. You will go through it and it will be resolved . Sending you positive !! healing vibes .

View 1 reply Cara Gavin Callanan Top contributor I’m not a doctor, but my thinking is that if it isn’t causing more pain, you’re not re-injuring it or otherwise over doing it. Lower drop shoes, like altras, will help strengthen the feet compared to cushiony, higher drop types like Hokas, so I would think that this switch would be good as long as your foot is ready for it.

body:
I am in my 3rd month of PF. There is a lot of conflicting information on the internet.  The newer approaches suggest active stretching, flat footware also called zero drop footware and wide toe boxes. These newer approaches seem to be working but time will tell. There is lots of video on You Tube which provide conflicting approaches. The newer approaches seem to be working for me but it is too soon to tell. I can say that my symptoms are not getting worse, so that is somewhat encouraging. See less

body:
I took a BIG chance. I bought boots I’ve eyeing for several months online. I wasn’t sure if I was going to need to size up but the size said 8-8.5 and I’m usually an 8W. Luckily, they were available in wide. They are very supportive yet roomy in the toe box. The arch support is amazing. First time, and prob last, I’ve ever spent this much but I have no regrets!!! Wish I could afford a pair in each color!!! Anyone else shop TAOS shoes???

View 1 reply Answer as Justin Muriel Stonewall posted to Plantar Fasciitis Talk and Tips Support Group For those of you getting the series of shockwave treatments, not the orthotripsy shockwave, make sure you tell whomever is delivering the treatment to push hard not being afraid to hurt you. The first 3 I received did nothing. The last two I told them not even to look at my face contorting in pain and to just apply pressure. The 5th treatment I got I actually feel a difference. Not 100% by any means but I can walk on my heel without terrible pain. I see him next week and will give the doctor an update. I’m hoping to get another treatment or 2. In the meantime, I finally hit the MRI and will have those results to confirm that it is only of and heel spur or something else. See less All reactions:
Comment PinkLynx7449 Keep us posted
April Alcantara Rising contributor Hoping for a good mri result for you.

2 week post op update
I had a scarf and akin osteotomy 2 weeks and just want to give a little update.
I had my 2 week follow up with surgeon yesterday and he is very happy with progress.
Week 1-2 are for wound healing and 2-6 for bone healing.
He changed bandage to a much smaller dressing and I will also continue to wear boot and use 1 or 2 crutches until my 6 week appointment. I need to keep foot elevated for next 4 weeks and continue with toe exercises.

Dear nice people on Facebook,
I would be super grateful for advice please. I had an NHS operation on my left foot in October and the position of the big toe was such that it caused the second and third toe to solidify into hammertoes. I was devastated. The pain was much worse after the op than before it. I have a chance to have revision surgery (also on the NHS) but am frankly terrified that it could make it even worse. The surgeon seems to want to correct only the hammertoes but the position of the big toe and pressure it exerts will, I believe, cause those corrected hammertoes to recur. At the moment I have found a way to reduce the pain but after walking a little the second toe really throbs. I am 58 and not sure whether the risk of more surgery is worth it. The surgeon has a good reputation.
Thanks in advance for any feedback.
Hugs and prayers,
Athena
comments: Lisa Bucherati Mather This looks totally misaligned. I wouldn’t think he could fix just the toes without fixing the big toe first! I wouldn’t consider a second opinion.

I wanted to share this for anyone in here who feels like there’s no way out of plantar fasciitis right now.
It can get better. But it takes time, consistency, and patience.
I’ve been dealing with it since January 2025. Like a lot of people, I tried to push through it at first - kept running until around August when it finally stopped me in my tracks and had me limping.
At that point, I honestly didn’t know if I’d run again. And running is a huge part of who I am.
I had to stop everything for a while - no running, no hiking, barely walking. After having just run a marathon months before, that was a tough place to be.
I tried a lot of the “quick fixes”:
massage guns, needling, complete rest, red light therapy… none of them solved it (some made it worse, some just emptied my wallet).
What actually helped was going right back to basics and committing to it:
Never going barefoot, even when popping to the loo at night. Oofos sliders have been my lifesaver. I still wear them inside, as well as the trainers style for popping out. Sorry they're ugly but in the first few months they're all my feet could handle.
Building strength from my hips all the way down to my feet.
30 minutes of strengthening and mobility every single day.
Sticking with it even when progress felt slow.
These are some of the things I did consistently:
– Toe control work (lifting big toe while others stay down, then switching)
– Arch lifts (keeping feet flat but lifting the arch without scrunching toes)
– Calf raises (progressively adding weight over time)
– Resistance band work for hips (rotations + marches)
– Lower back/hip stretches (lying twists, 30 seconds each side)
– Calf stretches - gentle (holding for 2 mins each side)
Everything done as 3 x 15 on each side.
As I got stronger, I increased weights and moved into more unilateral work to build balance and stability side to side.
Now, 15 months on, I’m back running.
It’s not perfect yet! And nowhere near where I was just 2 years ago but it's coming and I'm finally seeing the light!
I’m doing run/walk intervals (9:30 run / 1:00 walk for 40 minutes, 3 x per week), improving week by week.
And most importantly… the pain isn’t controlling my life anymore.
I’m also back to barefoot strength work and slowly reintroducing normal shoes.
If you’d told me before Christmas that I’d be here, I wouldn’t have believed you.
So if you’re in that stage where it feels endless — I get it. I was there not long ago.
But there is a way through it.
Stay consistent, rebuild properly, and give it time

After having my MIS bunion surgery on 25.06.05. I felt pretty surprisingly good and was able to weight bare pretty much straight away...
But my "ego" thought I wouldn't need any pain relief, but that's because the "nerve block" is very deceptive and tricks you into thinking you're Superman until it starts to wear off!
Prior to the surgery I quite liked the "falling asleep" bit of the anesthetic, but after surgery I felt like my body was involved in the custody battle between hunger and nausea...but that has now gone.
There have been some quite strong throbbing sensations in my foot at night making sleep the biggest challenge I'm facing at the moment... especially as I don't naturally sleep on my back.
Even though I shuffle kinda like one of those walking dead zombies; I've upped my vitamin D with K2 intake and will continue with my vitamin c & zinc among others.
I choose the MIS method, because I'm quite active and want gentle movement and mobility to be a part of my recovery process, but
I'm writing these posts to "share" but not compare...but so far despite the early challenges I have no regrets.
I've read a range of posts and experiences and have come to realise we're all individual, so it's important to stay as comfortable as possible and respect the healing process.
Look after yourselves my friends.
P.S. If this goes smoothly I will get the left one done as well. See less

Recovery update after my June 11, 2025 bunion repair (Lapiplasty – correction of the bunion with Calcaneal Bone Graft and lesser toe corrections):
NW bearing for 4 weeks to walking in boot for 4 weeks. Then transitioned to a tennis shoe for 12 weeks of physical therapy and returned to work in October on modified duty — straight 8-hour days to get back in a steel toe boot.
After returning, I started having pain under the outside of my ankle toward pinkie toe. I saw my surgeon in December 2025 and she believed it was likely stiffness from recovery and advised me to continue stretching.
At my February appointment, the pain was still present. Now, she thinks it could possibly be a tendon issue under the ankle to stop stretching. It seemed to aggravate it more.  She put me in an ankle support brace and have another appointment scheduled in May. I’ve been released back to my normal 12-hour shifts with the brace in a steel toe boot but no overtime until we see if the tendon improves. If not, the next step will likely be an MRI to see exactly what’s going on. Still have the pain when walking without the brace to now my 3rd lesser toe wanting to cramp up in the evenings. One thing to another lol
The bunion correction itself is doing well — just continuing to work through this ankle issue. See less
All reactions:

Comment as Justin Donna Kay Arrington posted to bunion surgery / foot surgery support group Rising contributor Hey everyone! It’s been a while since I’ve posted, so I wanted to share an update. I’m about 5 months out from Lapiplasty (Akin osteotomy, calcaneal bone graft, and correction of three lesser toes). I been back to work for 2 weeks in steel toe shoes on modified hours and restrictions and continuing physical therapy to work on strength, flexibility, and balance. Still have some swelling and stiffness, but I’m seeing slow, steady progress. I go back Dec 18 to see if I can be released with no restrictions back to 12 hour shifts. For anyone earlier in recovery, hang in there — it really is a journey, but every little milestone counts. See less All reactions:

Hey everybody.  I am a new member and I have lots of questions.  I will go ahead and ask a few now and hopefully start to get an understanding.  I realize I should probably see a podiatrist, and I am getting close to that point…but I am a typical stubborn man.  Which brings me to my first question.  Is it common for men to have bunions?  It seems like most or all posts I see are from women.
Next question.  Is the surgery worth it?  I am not in a crazy amount of pain while wearing what I wear.  I don’t have a lot of good shoe options.  Hiking boots are too painful.  The bunion sneakers I had are a broken ankle waiting to happen, very little support.  What I found to be most comfortable for me are crocs and these extra wide walmart sneakers that cost like $20.

I posted a picture of my nasty foot.  Would that be considered a bad bunion?  Looks bad to me but idk.  Sorry, my feet are gross.  I work outside and am on my feet all day.  My wife wants me to get a pedicure but to tell you the truth, I don’t like people seeing them.

Review of the Sklar Bunionectomy
Hey! I was born with bunions (thanks Dad for that one!) and underwent the Sklar bunionectomy with an Akin as well as tailor's bunion surgery by Drs. Sklar at Foot First Podiatry back in about 2018 (not at the same time). These pics are the most recent I have from this past year of follow-up when I finally got some screws out. When I was getting bunion surgery, I really wanted to be pain free with good function, but as a young woman I also wanted something that would be cosmetically beautiful. At the time, I was also finishing up undergrad, so I really wanted to spend the least amount of time in bed with a cast on. I went to one podiatrist who wanted to fuse me with an incision over the top of my foot and make me nonweightbearing for weeks. My dad begged me to visit Foot First and get a second opinion after hearing about them on the radio. I walked (limped) out of the surgery center with a surgical shoe and in about 3 weeks I was in gym shoes again. I can honestly say that this surgery was life-changing. I loved the doctors and their work so much that I went back to school and recently graduated as a podiatrist, too! Hoping that one day I can change lives as much as they changed mine
All reactions:

body:
Had my post op appointment yesterday, 10 days post op. Bandages off and stitches were taken out and cleared to get my foot wet which I done for the first time this morning. Still rather swollen around my big toe, not in any great deal of pain now, just the odd tender feeling. Been encouraged to walk as normal as I can when in orthopaedic sandal/shoe (perhaps walked around a little too much yesterday). Any tips for this part of recovery and how not to become too complacent? I would not want to hinder any progress further down the line but also want to make sure I am making the right progress for now! See less
All reactions:
comments: View more answers Nikki Brandenburg Schumacher Follow I’m about the same as you … 10 days post op. But do they my stitches out until July 7th. Still in boot. Just wanted to say your foot is looking great!

View 1 reply Vicki Corbin Top contributor Your foot looks great! Listen to your body and if you start feeling more pain then you should stop. My doctor gave me the ok to start walking a little after my 2 week visit but I was worried that my foot wouldn’t fuse so waited a month. My doctor didn’t give me the ok to go to the grocery store until 10 weeks out because of all the walking we do shopping. I did go then and felt some discomfort after but it went away after a couple of hours.
Answer as Justin

body:
Scared but going forward with my surgery - on July 16th after having bunions since I have been in my teens. I have cancelled the surgery before out of fear of the long recovery.
I am having a lapidus with 1st metatarsal arthrodesis and midfoot fusion on my right foot. Apparently need a bone graft as well.
I believe I will be non weight bearing for at least 8 weeks - maybe longer.
I live alone except for my 2 teens who are in summer camp for a month after the surgery - which is a good thing.
I have a knee scooter to get around the apt.
For those of you who were non weight bearing for so long-  what were the biggest challenges that you needed someone to help with?      I live in NYC so will likely order food, groceries to be delivered.
I am trying to figure out if I should hire someone to stay with me the first few weeks or just need someone to pop in a few times a day to check on me, help with meals, etc .
I will have friends visiting me  but since it’s the summer and many are away I want to make sure I have dedicated help if it’s needed.
Thanks for any advice you can offer! See less
All reactions:
comments: View more answers Ali Jane Top contributor Well done for getting the surgery! I’ve just had my second bunion op two weeks ago and both times I was non WB for at least 2 weeks. I batch cooked and cleaned before I went in. I had help with shopping since getting home and apart from overdoing it with a day out, all has been fine. If you have a toilet and bathroom on your floor you will be fine. I showered and washed my hair the following day and hopped about my home with no issues. I showered with a bag in my foot. It’s not as bad as it seems, you will be fine.

Tamra Fakhoorian I had fam ck in on me every day for the first few days, then gradually spread it out to 2x a week at the end of my second week. Frozen meals are the way to go. Don't do like me and order a pizza delivered on Day 4. It took me several minutes to get to the door, then I had to figure out how to carry the thing back to my bed!

body:
I am disabled but desperately need advice. I’m sorry if I may have asked this question before but I need help with slippers. I stand up to just pull my lower clothes up and down to use the commode this is the only time I stand but my slipper’s are killing my feet so much I’m in agony. I wear Velcro fastening extra wide non slip slippers but after 3 days of wearing them they gradually started to feel tighter across the whole foot especially as I have a high instep. When I loosen the strap then the back of slipper goes to loose but around the toes they are too tight and literally burn my feet. I’m looking for non slip easy fastening slippers as I can’t go on like this. I woke up in terrible pain and my first thought was oh no I’ve got to put those torture slippers on. I haven’t worn shoes for a year as always worn slippers out. Please any advice I’m really desperate. Thanks See less
All reactions:
comments: View more comments Ann Cotter The running company shoe store is great. They help find good supportive shoes and you can take them home and if you don't like them you can return them. Find a shoe store if don't live in Lincoln, NE that sells good quality shoes and have them find you good supportive shoes. You are damaging your feet by not wearing good quality support shoes.

I have drop foot in both feet but for months I’ve had terrible pain in my feet mainly left one . It’s making my walking so unstable and I’ve already fallen and broke my Neck of femur. My feet hurt in bed and all day long . It can be all over or just the ankles and underneath the feet. I’ve tried braces from orthotics but they hurt too much. My Dr doesn’t do anything as I don’t tolerate meds so every night I put Deep Heat on my feet but that’s not helping either. I’m really fed up with as I have to have my husband walk behind me all the time encase I fall. Even when I’m in my wheelchair when I have to go outside my feet hurt so much that I now hate using my wheelchair. Sorry for the long post but I just don’t know what to do. Thanks See less All reactions:

I have worn BIrkenstocks for years. And even after getting PF in my left foot from overuse from taking on a new activity and trying to do it 3x per week to 6 days per week because I wanted to learn if quickly so I thought. (Golf) I still found these sandals more comfortable than any other shoe I owned except my New Balance runners with custom orthotics.
Podiatrists view Birkenstocks as a solid, supportive option for everyday wear. They praise the cork footbed, which generally helps with arch pain, flat feet, and bunions. However, experts warn they aren’t for everyone; their rigid, firm design can aggravate severe foot conditions or fail to offer enough shock absorption.
What Podiatrists Like
Arch Support: The built-in arch support prevents the foot from collapsing. It acts as a helpful "cradle" for people with mild plantar fasciitis or flat feet.
Deep Heel Cup: It cradles the heel, stabilizing the foot and controlling overpronation.
Roomy Toe Box: The wide, open design allows toes to spread naturally, preventing deformities like hammer toes.
Zero Drop: Most classic styles are flat (no elevation from heel to toe), which is better for overall spinal alignment than heeled footwear See less

comments: View more comments GentleBlueberry6862 Yes, they are pricey but it is so worth it. I LOVE my Birkenstocks. I wear Boston and Arizona.

HumblePomegranate3609 Birkenstocks were one of the biggest contributors to my healing, I wear them all the time unless sleeping or running. I ended up with 3 pairs (indoor sandals, outdoor sandals and then closed toe)

Comment as Justin Jody Stevens posted to Plantar Fasciitis Talk and Tips Support Group My story: .I have had plantar for 1.5 years from over use  from learning golf . I also have worn orthotics for over a decade . I used to work for a chiro. So when my podiatrist said my orthotics are too flexible and I need more rigid ones I was highly skeptical. Why would I want more rigid I thought . Well you do . More on that later. I did the calf stretches, had  a cortisone injection & continued to golf (January- April) until i couldn’t do it anymore which is now  2 months later . Now it’s April I’m back from Palm Springs  and so I went back to my podiatrist. Got a 2 nd cortisone shot which they have stopped my pain immediately but the first one lasted 2 months. Ordered new orthotics and continued calf stretches. Luckily I’m not working so I’m able to rest it. I used to wear Birkenstocks for years no problem but now I feel I can only wear them for a couple hours . I dont get pain wearing my new orthotics to my surprise in my old New balance runners I’ve had for years. Cortisone immediately took all the pain away . The first shot only last 2 months but I continued to golf . The 2 nd shot has lasted for  2 months then some pain returned but not too bad at all and I’m still  progressing so I recommend getting them. Only 3 in a year is standard practice. This is such a slow healing process. I thought resting it would be all I would need . How people recover from this while working is beyond me. My podiatrist says 3-4 months before golfing again. I have progressed now to strengthening exercises like heel raise on my stairs. I’m 59 year old female. I joined a gym as it gets me to commit to doing exercises.  Riding a bike or elliptical is ok but not a treadmill. Podiatrist said start at 10 mins. I can now do it for 30 mins then I move on to weights. I never go barefoot as podiatrist recommended and I can tell it’s too much for my flat feet. I only have left foot PF. I swear calf stretches really help. When I have a flare I ice it right away and that works. So I am going on 3 months and pain has gone down to 3/10 from 8/10 and it’s way less frequent. My podiatrist will not recommend Hokas but yes to New Balance and Birkenstocks depending on how you feel . I also had met pads put in My orthotics for Morton’s neuroma on my left foot which stopped all that pain years ago so I continue to keep that in my new orthotics which took 3 weeks . I hope someone finds this useful. It’s not a life sentence it takes commitment and months to heal. Physio recommended taping it in the beginning of the pain which did help until it got too bad because I tried  to keep on golfing. Had to stop to let it heal. See less All reactions:

Comment View more comments Bob Meadows Top contributor Most of that is the opposite of what I did and I healed in 11 weeks. Cortisone shots do nothing for healing PF. In most cases it makes it worse. I wore orthotics for just 3 weeks then transitioned to zero-drop shoes. Altra Paradigm wide toe box zero-drop shoes. Stretching & strengthening exercises 3 times a day along with massaging my calves and feet were the most important thing for my healing. Also, nightly Epsom salt soaks helped a lot.

View all 11 replies Kelly Scott Tennis shoes wear out and breakdown. If you have had the same shoes for years you need new ones. Heel raises tighten your calf muscles, opposite of what you want. Heel drops and back to only neutral are more beneficial. You need to strengthen the intrinsic muscles in your foot.
View 1 reply Comment as Justin
conditions_mentioned: bunion, hammer toe, plantar fasciitis, flat feet, neuroma, overpronation
treatments_mentioned: cortisone, orthotics, taping, elevation, stretching, custom orthotics, rolling, arch support, epsom salt

Comment View more answers Andrea Speas Hagen Moderator All-star contributor Mine went away. Since you're 99% better, have you tried changing diet yet? Read the Sugar Impact Diet by JJ Virgin. I was at 95% with just a little annoyance daily. Chiro recommended this book. I followed her plan to a T and it was completely gone within the first week. I did follow her plan for 3 months but then went back to normal eating afterwards. Now I can tell when I have a LOT of stuff I shouldn't be (like Christmas) but it goes away again.

Carol Rose Good question. My foot definitely is better, but it's tender and unstable the first couple minutes in the morning. My tailbone area is bad also the first couple minutes, so am thinking there's a connection.

Comment Aron Kinzett All-star contributor I believe it does. But like any stress injury, it's creating a buffer zone between oneself and re injury. Which usually involves more strength or mobility. Nearly healed or plateauing usually means there is something missing to completely heal. I could be as simple as not having barefoot time or transitioning to minimalistic shoes.

Comment View more answers Jennifer Calow Bryant Bigby Three days was about how long it took me to it was an easier transition than I thought. Became easier after the first day and I got over the fear. And I actually had a nonunion and am 3 weeks out from reconstructive surgery and non weight bearing again.

Peggy Jones Pfister All-star contributor Your foot looks a lot like mine did pre-op, and I'm guessing from your incisions that we had similar surgeries. My doc's protocol was a bit different, so I was nwb a bit longer. I had a surgical splint for two weeks, hard cast for five weeks, then boot (but still nwb) for six weeks. Then I was allowed to transition to wb gradually, in a supportive athletic shoe. He had me do 25% wb in the first week, then 50% for two weeks, then 100%, as tolerated, so three weeks to transition. He instructed me to take it slower if I needed to, and he gave me mild pain meds for the transition, but I didn't need them. I would say your body will tell you how much time you need to transition. Best of luck to you!

How to get pain out of your feet.
There's one main way to get pain out of your feet. It's called self massage. Oh, some people will claim that orthotics are the way. Yes they do help and I sell custom orthotics... but that's not the main way. Others will talk about how pads they put in their shoes were helpful. Ok, fine. Some people go to podiatrists or doctors for cortisone shots. Good luck on that!
Ok, so here is what you do. Spend TIME massaging your own feet every single day. I teach this on Youtube. (I've taught millions of people over the last 15 years.) When massaging your feet, remember this key phrase: No pain, no gain! Yes, it's painful... like a workout! DIG out the pain with deep pressure! You can use your hands or you can use massage tools. After massaging your feet, STRETCH THEM! Do the work!
Here are some pictures to inspire you. Do the work! If you want to buy some massage tools, message me. Remember, the main way to get pain out of your feet is by doing self massage! (The simple things in life are the best but you have to make an effort!) You also have to massage and stretch your legs! This is because your feet are attached to them! The tension in your legs is affecting your feet!!! Do not forget this!
If you want a lesson from me online, let me know. You can also just watch videos on Youtube. (I'm not the only one teaching self foot massage but I'm the best.) It's free ok but you have to do the work so it's not really free. Nothing in life that's worth anything is free.
Have a great day!

comments: View more answers Denise Reynolds If you were in UK I honestly don’t think they would even consider surgery for your big toe- yours would I think be classed as mild. I can have surgery- mine are ‘extreme’ - lump is far bigger and my toe almost horizontal across my other toes. Even so I’m still considering whether to go ahead due to the recovery time/pain/ non weight bearing practicalities etc. it’s not straightforward. However, if the lump you mention is in your mid foot ( can’t really tell from photos) that might be a different story. Have you had x rays/scans? Seems to me like you need a confirmed diagnosis to be able to decide anything. I really feel for you that cost has to have a bearing on what you do! Thank goodness for the NHS ! Not perfect but compared to your situation it’s incredible

Comment View more answers Shontelle Kennedy Honestly I was so nervous about getting my feet done after reading a lot in this group as it seems a lot of people are mainly having negative stories. I ended up getting both feet done and it’s the best thing I have ever done for myself. I’m no longer in pain, I can wear sandals and cute heels with confidence as well as normal shoes!! It did take a long time for swelling to fully go away. I had bunions and bunionettes. I did them all at once. It was extremely painful for the first few weeks. I found Chinese acupuncture helped with healing and pain management. I got the surgery over a year ago and it’s great. I was a regular gym goer and eased back in and took my time before lifted heavy again. I think 8 months before I lifted heavy. Now I’m so glad I didn’t cancel the surgery!!

Betty Ann Mellon Egolf replied Melissa McBryde Do your research and get more than one opinion. I also am 52, super active and I have an almost 6 year old son that is a mini tornado from sun up until sun down. Your feet will get worse over time. I just had Lapiplasty with a calcaneal (heel) bone graft and the 2nd and 3rd toes set with pins in them to straighten them out. This was performed on my right foot. Tomorrow I get my pins out and hope to start trying a shoe instead of the walking boot. Today was my 6-week post op date. I am beyond thrilled that I had this done. There are so many resources out there. But don’t fall for all of the negative you read. People with success stories don’t always share them. Any pain I had initially was super manageable and completely tolerable. It’s just inconvenient, especially when you’re active and have kids. You’ll be glad you had the surgery!
Teresa Marion replied 1 Reply Answer as Justin

Comment as Justin Gina Marie posted to Minimally invasive bunion surgery - have questions? Get Answers! Rising contributor One week post MIS & the bottom of my big toe hurts really badly today. Yesterday surgeon unwrapped it and said it looked good - ok to take boot off for sleeping, still on for walking. Any thoughts? OTC meds? Or concern? All reactions:

Comment View more answers Mary Copouls I’m 3 weeks post op and still taking ibuprofen and Tylenol around the clock. When I had the dressing changed at 1 week, my foot was unbelievably tender. I was terrified for anything to touch it. A friend who is a PT recommended that I use a dry washcloth and gently rub it across my foot for a minute or so to wake up the nerves and shock them out of being on high alert. I did this a few times a day and it really helped with decreasing sensitivity. You could also try icing and elevating.

JDi Sano replied Lisa Tucker-Law I experienced increased pain when my doc removed my NWB cast/splint and sutures. Went into a walking boot. I believe I had more swelling with the activity and possibly less support? Eventually it resolved.

Is there any hope when you've had this fungus for 20 years, all 10 toes? It used to be manageable, but they've become worse, very crumbly and if I get them wet for too long some fall off, which is beyond depressing
I love swimming but don't do it much anymore. I've tried endless OTC, but no drugs because I've had some other health issues so didn't want to risk it.
All reactions:
comments: View more answers Shelley Gammon I had very similar. I finally beat it. I got 2 shoebox size plastic bins. Filled half way with warm water and then poured in a cup of bleach in each bin and soaked my feet (one in each bin) for 20 minutes. I did this for about 2 weeks. The nasty yellow nails, I was able to pull off with some effort. Underneath was a paper thin membrane over my flesh where the nails were. The small nails grew back quickly. My toes are pink and the nails are clear that are growing back. Big toe nail is about 2/3 grown in.

Liz Payne replied Frank Garcia Rising contributor I had all 10 of mine removed and nail matrix “burned”. I now have the freedom to go about my business barefoot without being self conscious. I’m my only regret is I didn’t do it sooner thinking it would get better with meds and other treatments.

Ok, another question about Birkenstocks! I’m finding my Arizonas really help my Achilles tendonitis and peroneal tendonitis (I loosened up the top strap and they are finally getting comfy!) and I do think they help my PF as well. My question is - do they help to strengthen your feet? It feels like you have to “grip” a bit with your toes which is similar to some of the strengthening exercises they had me do at PT. Comment as Justin Anthony Zagaroli posted to Plantar Fasciitis Talk and Tips Support Group If anyone is suffering from plantar fasciitis pain due to tight calves, I just purchased a slant board on Amazon and it has been a game-changer. Have not had any pain in almost 4 days now. Doing calf lowering stretches on the edges of steps puts lots of strain on the metatarsal and can create new problems in your forefoot/balls of feet. The slant board stretches your calves and evenly distributes the weight. You can start at a low incline and work your way up. All reactions:

Hi all, happy Sunday/Father’s Day. I had bilateral scarf/akin osteotomies with maestro/DSTR here in the UK last Monday, 15 June, so it’ll be a week tomorrow (thankfully it’s gone by quickly!).
I left hospital with both feet bandaged, with special post-surgery shoes to wear whenever weight-bearing, and crutches to use if needed. I have dissolving stitches and steristrips, and have strict instructions to keep the dressing and bandages in place until I see the surgeon on Thur… See more
All reactions:

Best time of year?
I recently had my left foot done because the pain was starting to impact my daily life. The recovery is going ok, I’ve got a bit of my energy back and I’m starting to think about getting the right foot done. It’s structurally worse than the left, but doesn’t usually hurt. Although I do think it’s causing issues with my knees.
Are there any advantages to getting surgery based on season? Spring was good, but it rained every time I had an appointment. The morning of my surgery we had to reroute due to flooding at 5am! Summer would be hot and would miss all the fun stuff. Fall has football, so that’s out
. Winter the weather could be bad making it hard to get around (plus someone will have to drive me and help me navigate the snow)
Getting it done by the end of the year will save me in cost since I’ve hit my deductible. But not sure dealing with winter and missing out on the holidays is worth the cost savings as follow-ups will continue through the next year anyway. Just the mental impact of likely sitting home alone on Christmas is kind of bumming me out and it’s only May!
All reactions:
comments: View more comments Jennifer Schumacher Roth All-star contributor I think whatever works for you. I had one done in February and the other in January. I live in the Northeast US and I hate winter so to me I preferred to be home and not missing out on summer vacation, etc, and my kids don't have a ton going on at that time of year either.
Lisa Clift Hogan January worked for me. Weather crappy and by the time it was getting nice I was definitely getting around just fine.

body:
Today, I had my one week post-op visit.  My surgeon was pleased with my progress.  I had bunion (osteotomy and arthrodesis), hammertoe correction, and neuroma removal surgery.  He did some work on my third toe, too, because it was deviating from where it should be.  I’ve been extremely diligent about elevating my foot and icing periodically.   I’m cleared to drive in my surgical sandal and to heel walk without crutches, etc.

I had my bunion, hammertoe and neuroma surgery today.  So far, the only time my foot hurt was right after I woke up from the anesthesia. I did not have a nerve block, so maybe this won’t be so bad?  Percocet is doing the pain control. Funny story - the discharge nurse said to not get on Amazon. They’ve had patients wake up from surgery and buy a thousands of dollars of silly stuff on Amazon and not remember doing it.

Wondering if anyone has gone back for hardware removal after several years? I had my original surgery 3 years ago (Lapidus) and have three titanium screws. I have always been able to sort of “feel” where one of the top screws is, but it never bothered me except if I wear shoes like sandals with a strap that directly hits the incision spot for an extended period of time. I had no complications with the original surgery (except the long, long, recovery- 9 weeks nwb). Lately, the screw has really started to bother me and is painful to the point where I think it’s affecting my gait some days. I’m wondering what the recovery for screw removal like this would be? I’m in a very different living situation now than I was three years ago (have a flight of 19 steep stairs to get into my apartment, no family support system nearby anymore) so I’m worried about the recovery of it all. My other foot also probably needs surgery at some point but I don’t think I can swing another 8+ week recovery right now. Hoping the screw removal would be much easier? See less

comments: View more answers Dawn Lewis All-star contributor I had my screws removed 2 years after. You definitely don't need 8 weeks for the screw removal. Depending on your job it would be a few days to 2 weeks. If you're on your feet it would be closer to 2 weeks according to what the surgeon told me. I ended up with 4 weeks off because my incision was slow healing because I didn't listen to the directions and did not take it easy and some of my stitches popped and I got a bit of an infection. It was pretty painless so it was hard to just sit around and do nothing. I ended up not even taking any pain meds and was walking pretty normal right after surgery.

Answer as Justin Gabriela Jackson posted to bunion surgery / foot surgery support group Hi everyone! I had lapidus surgery on 11/17, non weight bearing for 8 weeks. Just got my stitches out and cast off yesterday, and they gave me a boot to wear. I’m allowed to take it off for short periods of time, but must sleep with it. They warned me it would be painful at first because my foot can’t fully bend to 90-degrees yet. But it’s so much more painful than I thought, almost unbearable to keep it on for more than 1 hr. My feet ache on the bottom as they would if I was wearing a really uncomfortable shoe without arch support, or a super tight ice skate or ski boot. Anyone else have a similar experience? Any suggestions? Thanks! See less

This is my 7th surgery on right foot, I've learned a few things over the years! I have to wear my boot 24/7.

I was told non weight bearing for two weeks and to wear the boot anytime my foot was going to be near the ground. After two weeks, I could barely touch my foot to the ground, but a week later I’m able to walk on it in the boot. The boot is very uncomfortable but mostly where it hits my incision. When the boot is off, I’d be doing a lot of foot wiggling to get it to 90 degrees and more flexible. I’d also ice it in case it’s swelling.

When the heel bone drifts too far outward — creating the valgus hindfoot alignment that drives progressive flatfoot deformity and places destructive stress on the posterior tibial tendon — one of the most powerful surgical tools available to correct this fundamental structural problem is the Medializing Calcaneal Osteotomy.
This procedure involves cutting the calcaneus — the heel bone — through a precisely placed lateral incision an… See more

Flatfoot Deformity is one of the most common structural foot conditions affecting Lancaster County residents across every age group — and one of the most frequently misunderstood in terms of when it requires professional attention and when it can be safely monitored.
A flatfoot deformity occurs when the medial longitudinal arch of the foot collapses, causing the entire inner border of the foot to make contact with the ground during … See more

So I'm starting to think it's me. I started the insurance business in 2009 at a call center. Within 2 months I could bind a home and auto policy within 7-10 minutes on a recorded line.
Fast forward to 2026... it took me over an hour to accept a payment yesterday, I did 3 quotes with 8 carriers through my aggregator that took about an hour each and couldn't be completed with multiple carriers due to system glitches.
I feel like technology didn't take a step back it fell off a cliff.
The page scrolling, errors, multiple platforms for each carrier, lack of information populating and crashes have made this job more time consuming. I enter information and the carrier encrypts it which causes a redundancy.
Did something in the industry change or am I just hitting that senior period of my life where I hate technology? Like an athlete who can't dunk or bench 315 anymore?
Everyday it's harder to get excited about work, even in the making money calls. I'm too young(poor) to retire and to old to change industries.
And don't get me started on the service center attitudes and language skills. See less

Comment as Justin Plantar Fasciitis Talk and Tips Support Group Gabriella Cserna Rising contributor IF NOTHING ELSE HAS HELPED YOUR PLANTAR FASCIITIS, READ THIS. I can only share what worked for me. My plantar fasciitis became so severe that I could barely walk. It hurt even when I was lying in bed. I tried the night splint, massage, stretching, walking barefoot, and the advice to keep moving. In my case, all of that either didn’t help or made it worse. Out of desperation, I taped my foot very firmly using a YouTube taping method and kept it taped continuously for about three weeks, replacing the tape when needed. For me, complete support and rest were what finally allowed it to heal. Today I have no pain at all. I’m not saying this will work for everyone, but if you’ve already tried everything else, taping might be worth a try. It completely changed the outcome for me. Link! https://youtu.be/DTmcVzrka1Q?is=9PAMxOAoW2Vnbhxq All reactions:
Comment View more comments Ricci Cottrell Kennar The tape definitely helps your foot feel more supported and eased the pain. Wondering which tape you went with. Stretch or no stretch?
View 1 reply Carolyn Gamble-Speaker Follow Strong Arch Support fixes it. That is what you did with the tape.
